title: Steak tartare
subtitle: Raw minced beef with capers, cornichons, and egg yolk
excerpt: Finely hand-chopped raw beef fillet seasoned with capers, cornichons, shallot, Dijon mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and a raw egg yolk. Mixed at the table or assembled in the kitchen, served immediately with toast. A dish of technique, freshness, and confidence.
sourceNotes: Steak tartare appears in French bistro menus from at least the early twentieth century, though the combination of raw meat, condiments, and egg is documented across European cuisine from the nineteenth century. The name's connection to the Tartars is largely apocryphal. Escoffier includes a version in 'Le Guide Culinaire' (1903).

content.text: Steak tartare is a dish where sourcing, freshness, and technique carry everything. Buy the best beef fillet you can find from a butcher you trust, on the day you plan to serve it. Do not use pre-minced beef: it has too much surface area, deteriorates quickly, and lacks the right texture. Hand-chop to small but irregular dice (about 3 mm) rather than grinding. The rough, not-quite-uniform texture is part of the dish.

The seasoning is assertive: Dijon mustard for sharpness, Worcestershire sauce for depth, capers and cornichons for acid and crunch, and the egg yolk to bind and enrich. Mix it at the table if you want drama; mix it in the kitchen if you want control. Either way, serve immediately, the meat deteriorates within minutes once mixed.

items.name: Beef fillet
items.prepNote: very fresh, hand-chopped to fine dice

content.text: Trim any sinew or fat from the {{beef-fillet}} of fillet. Using a sharp knife, slice the beef into thin planks, stack the planks, and cut across into strips, then across again into a very fine dice, about 3 mm pieces. Do not use a food processor. Place in a bowl over ice and refrigerate until needed.

Remove the beef from the fridge. Add the {{dijon-mustard}} of mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, {{capers}}, {{cornichons}}, {{shallot}}, and {{parsley-flat}} of parsley. Season well with salt and pepper. Mix briefly with a fork, do not overwork the meat. Taste and adjust seasoning. Make a small indent in the top of each portion and place an {{egg-yolks}} egg yolk in each. Serve immediately with toast.

items.fix: Mix and serve within 5 minutes of combining; keep the chopped meat over ice until the moment of plating
items.cause: Mixed too far in advance or left at room temperature
items.symptom: Meat has turned grey and soft

items.fix: Hand-chop to a visible dice, not a paste; the rough cut retains structure and drains less
items.cause: Meat was pre-minced or chopped too finely

items.fix: Season assertively, raw beef needs more salt than cooked; add a few drops of hot sauce and taste again
items.cause: Not enough salt, or condiments added without tasting

items.fix: Dice very small; alternatively, rinse the diced shallot in cold water for 30 seconds and pat dry to mellow the sharpness

content.text: Steak tartare is the classic French brasserie raw dish. It sits on menus alongside the half-dozen oysters and the jambon-beurre baguette, all confident simple food. It has a theatre to it. In classic service the dish is built at the table. The condiments come on the side and the diner is asked about seasoning. The dish needs trust in the sourcing and in the cook. It rewards that trust with something cooking cannot match. The clean, pure flavour of very good beef.
